## Bead ID: ralph-burning-TBD

## Goal

Make artifact producer metadata unambiguous by always including both the requested (resolved target) and actual (adapter-reported) backend/model, so readers can tell at a glance what was planned vs what ran.

## Problem

Currently `RecordProducer::Agent` in `src/contexts/workflow_composition/panel_contracts.rs:252-261` has:
- `backend_family` / `model_id` — always set to the **resolved target** (what was requested), overwritten in `src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs:235-236`
- `adapter_reported_backend_family` / `adapter_reported_model_id` — only populated when the actual backend **differs** from the target, and skipped during serialization when None

This means artifact JSON always shows the requested model as the producer, with no indication of whether it actually ran on that model. When they match (the common case), the adapter_reported fields are absent, making it impossible to distinguish "matched" from "not tracked."

## Changes Required

1. **Always populate `adapter_reported_backend_family` and `adapter_reported_model_id`** in `src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs:219-234` — remove the conditional that only sets them on mismatch. The adapter always reports what it used; pass it through unconditionally.

2. **Rename fields in `RecordProducer::Agent`** for clarity:
   - `backend_family` → `requested_backend_family`
   - `model_id` → `requested_model_id`
   - `adapter_reported_backend_family` → `actual_backend_family`
   - `adapter_reported_model_id` → `actual_model_id`

3. **Remove `skip_serializing_if`** on the actual fields — they should always be present in serialized artifacts.

4. **Update `agent_record_producer()`** in `src/contexts/workflow_composition/mod.rs:197-219` to pass both requested and actual values unconditionally.

5. **Update Display impl** for `RecordProducer` to show actual model (since that's what readers care about), e.g. `agent:claude/claude-opus-4-6` when they match, `agent:claude/claude-opus-4-6 (requested codex/gpt-5.4)` when they differ.

6. **Update tests** that assert on producer fields to use the new field names.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Artifact JSON always contains both requested and actual backend/model
- When they match, both fields have the same value (no ambiguity)
- When they differ, both are visible without needing to cross-reference raw backend files
- Existing artifact deserialization still works (serde defaults for renamed fields)

## Non-Goals

- Changing the raw backend output format
- Retroactively fixing old artifacts
close_reason: Merged in PR #98: renamed RecordProducer fields to requested/actual with backward-compat deserialization

title: Split live run state from .ralph-burning audit history and protect resume-critical files
description: Keep .ralph-burning reviewable and git-trackable without letting backend cleanup break active or resumable runs. Move authoritative mutable project/workspace state out of the repo cleanup blast radius, keep .ralph-burning as the audit mirror, stop reading resume-critical files from that mirror, and mirror only the small durable review/history files instead of runtime logs or temp state.
acceptance_criteria: live project prompt/config/snapshot/journal reads no longer depend on .ralph-burning; .ralph-burning still receives durable human-reviewable history such as project metadata, journal entries, and review artifacts; runtime logs, temp files, sessions, and other bulky mutable state are not mirrored into the tracked audit surface; deleting .ralph-burning/projects/<id> no longer makes an intact live run unrecoverable; project creation and prompt updates persist both live state and the audit mirror without regressing minimal-flow behavior
close_reason: Completed

title: Profile stub-backend CLI wait sources and identify the real bottleneck
description:
## Goal

Identify what actually dominates wall-clock time in the slow stub-backend CLI lifecycle and conformance tests so the next optimization step is based on measured evidence rather than profile assumptions.

## Problem

Release-mode benchmarking did not materially reduce the hot test runtime: `cli_run_start_releases_lock_on_error` still took 65.63s in release test time versus 69.65s in debug, and the broader CLI/conformance suites were slower in release. That strongly suggests the main delay is not ordinary CPU-bound debug overhead.

Current evidence points to waits rather than compute:
- `strace` showed 67.7% of syscall time in `futex`
- `wait4` accounted for another 10.6%
- Total syscall time was only about 0.53s while wall clock was 67-85s

The next step is to isolate the real wait source in the harness or runtime.

## Investigation Scope

1. Reproduce the slow-path measurements for the single lifecycle test and at least one suite-level target.
2. Attribute the dominant wall-clock delay to concrete sources such as:
- explicit sleeps or timeout defaults in stub-mode helpers
- subprocess spawn / git checkpoint overhead
- polling loops, synchronization cadence, or runtime scheduling
- repeated prompt/render/setup work that is hidden by instant-returning stubs
3. Add inspectable instrumentation or targeted profiling hooks so future optimization work can rerun the diagnosis deterministically.
4. Recommend the highest-leverage implementation change, with the expected impact on the under-15-second lifecycle target.

## Suggested Verification Paths

```bash
nix develop -c cargo test --locked --features test-stub --test cli cli_run_start_releases_lock_on_error -- --exact
nix develop -c cargo test --locked --features test-stub --test cli conformance_full_suite_passes -- --exact
```

Use `strace`, tracing spans, targeted timing logs, or similar inspectable diagnostics. Avoid ad hoc one-off scripts unless they become checked-in harness support.

## Files

- `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs`
- `src/cli/`
- `src/contexts/conformance_spec/`
- related test harness helpers and tracing/logging code

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
Reproduces the single lifecycle test and at least one suite-level slow-path benchmark with recorded timings.
Attributes the dominant wall-clock delay to concrete sources such as sleeps/timeouts, subprocess waits, synchronization cadence, or equivalent measurable causes.
Adds inspectable instrumentation, tracing, or profiling support that can be rerun deterministically for this diagnosis.
Identifies at least one actionable optimization candidate with expected impact toward the under-15-second lifecycle target.
`nix build` passes on the final tree.

title: final_review reviewers systematically disagree on amendments, causing arbiter overhead and round oscillation
description:
## Observed Pattern

Across 11 completion rounds, reviewer-1 (codex/gpt-5.5-xhigh) and reviewer-2 (codex/gpt-5.3-codex-spark-xhigh) systematically disagreed on amendment accept/reject votes. Reviewer-1 proposed 3-4 findings in several rounds, reviewer-2 rejected reviewer-1 findings while accepting its own, and the arbiter repeatedly selected a subset. The run was on track to hit force-complete rather than naturally converge.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Run a final_review panel with two reviewers that systematically vote against each other's proposed amendments.
2. Let the run continue for several completion rounds.
3. Observe repeated split votes, arbiter invocations, and a steady nonzero amendment count instead of convergence.

## Why This Is a Problem

Repeated arbiter firing adds runtime overhead, and steady low-count amendment disagreement causes round oscillation. Stronger and weaker reviewers are treated as peers even when the stronger reviewer is consistently finding more issues.

## Possible Mitigations

- Add configurable weighted voting for stronger reviewers.
- Add explicit self-vote handling for a reviewer's own amendment.
- Detect repeated arbiter oscillation and force-complete early with a clear journal note.
- Use smaller reviewer panels after low amendment counts persist.
- Use phase-scoped reviewers so weaker models can vote or arbitrate without always proposing.

## Acceptance Criteria

- A clear journal-event signature for oscillating-with-arbiter detection.
- One mitigation ships behind a config flag, likely convergence detection.
- Regression test exercises the oscillation pattern with deterministic stub reviewers.
- nix build passes; cargo test && cargo clippy -- -D warnings && cargo fmt --check pass.

title: Guard drift_still_satisfies_requirements against PromptReview quorum override
description:
drift_still_satisfies_requirements (engine.rs) applies effective_min_override uniformly to all stage types including PromptReview. The PromptReview resume path never sets this override and documents that prompt-review does NOT degrade on BackendExhausted, but the function signature accepts it. A future caller could silently weaken PromptReview quorum. Fix: ignore effective_min_override in the PromptReview arm or assert if non-None is passed.

## Acceptance Criteria

title: Run final_review panel reviewers and voters in parallel using tokio::JoinSet
description:
## Goal

Run the final_review panel reviewers concurrently instead of sequentially, and do the same for the voting phase. This cuts wall-clock time roughly in half for the 2-reviewer default panel.

## Context

Currently in final_review.rs, both the proposal phase (line ~282) and the voting phase iterate over panel members sequentially with a for loop, awaiting each reviewer/voter one at a time. Since reviewers are independent (they don't need each other's output), they can run in parallel via tokio::JoinSet or tokio::join!.

## Changes Required

### 1. Parallelize proposal phase
Replace the sequential for loop over panel.reviewers with concurrent spawning using tokio::JoinSet.

### 2. Parallelize voting phase
Same pattern for the voting loop.

### 3. Preserve event ordering
Journal events must remain correct per-reviewer.

### 4. Handle partial failures
BackendExhausted reviewers should be skipped while others complete.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Reviewers run concurrently via tokio::JoinSet or equivalent
- Voters run concurrently
- BackendExhausted handling still works
- Journal events are correct
- Cancellation token cancels all concurrent tasks
- No data races
- Existing tests pass
close_reason: Already implemented — both proposal and voting phases use FuturesUnordered for concurrent reviewer invocation. Result processing is sequential by necessity (journal seq counter), but that's fast I/O, not API calls.

title: Support per-member model overrides in panel backend specs
description:
## Goal

Allow each panel member (reviewer, voter, completer) to specify its own model independently, so panels like final_review can mix different models from the same backend family.

## Problem

Panel backend specs are plain backend family names: `["codex", "codex"]`. The model for each role is resolved from a single `backends.<family>.role_models.<role>` setting. When two panel members use the same backend family, they always get the same model — there is no way to say "codex slot 1 uses gpt-5.4, codex slot 2 uses gpt-5.3-codex-spark".

This became a real problem when Claude credits ran out: replacing the Opus reviewer with a second codex entry forced both reviewers to the same model, overwriting the gpt-5.4 reviewer with spark.

## Proposed Solution

Extend `PanelBackendSpec` to support an optional inline model override:

```
# Current (family only)
final_review.backends = ["codex", "codex"]

# Proposed (family with optional model override)  
final_review.backends = ["codex/gpt-5.4-xhigh", "codex/gpt-5.3-codex-spark-xhigh"]

# Or with the optional marker
final_review.backends = ["codex/gpt-5.4-xhigh", "?codex/gpt-5.3-codex-spark-xhigh"]
```

The `family/model` syntax is parsed in `PanelBackendSpec::from_str`. When a model is specified inline, it takes precedence over `backends.<family>.role_models.<role>`. When no model is specified (bare family name), the existing resolution chain applies.

## Changes Required

### 1. Extend PanelBackendSpec parsing
**File:** `src/shared/domain.rs` (~line 494, `FromStr` impl)

Parse `family/model` syntax. Store the optional model override in the enum variants.

### 2. Update BackendSelection
**File:** `src/shared/domain.rs`

`BackendSelection` already has `model_override: Option<String>`. Wire the inline model from PanelBackendSpec into this field during resolution.

### 3. Update panel resolution
**File:** `src/contexts/agent_execution/policy.rs` (~line 131)

When resolving panel members to concrete targets, prefer the inline model override over the role_models config lookup.

### 4. Update config set CLI
**File:** `src/contexts/workspace_governance/config.rs`

The `apply_string_list` function and `config set` validation should accept the new `family/model` format.

### 5. Update Display impl
**File:** `src/shared/domain.rs` (~line 485)

`PanelBackendSpec::Display` should render the model when present: `codex/gpt-5.4` or `?codex/gpt-5.3-codex-spark`.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Panel specs support `family/model` syntax: `["codex/gpt-5.4", "codex/gpt-5.3-codex-spark"]`
- Inline model overrides take precedence over role_models config
- Bare family names (no `/`) continue to work as before
- Optional marker `?` works with the new syntax: `?codex/gpt-5.4`
- `config set` and `config show` handle the new format
- Serde round-trip works (serialize/deserialize)
- Existing tests pass
- cargo test && cargo clippy && cargo fmt --check

title: Add comment documenting shadowed task binding in try_reconcile_success
description:
## Goal

Add a clarifying comment in `try_reconcile_success` (daemon_loop.rs) documenting the reliance on variable shadowing for correctness.

## Problem

In `try_reconcile_success`, `is_reconciliation_only_retry` uses `task.failure_class` from the pre-claim snapshot. The pre-claim `task` binding shadows the outer `task` parameter. This is correct but subtle — the correctness depends on the shadow being in scope at the second use site inside the `match outcome` block.

## Fix

Add a brief comment at both use sites explaining that `task` refers to the pre-claim snapshot (with failure_class intact) rather than the post-claim `active_task` (where failure is cleared).

## Files

- `src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs`

## Acceptance Criteria

- Comment explains the shadowing invariant at the relevant binding sites
- No functional change

title: Set final review panel defaults: 1 required reviewer, optional Claude/Codex-spark, Codex 5.4 arbiter
description:
## Goal

Change the default final review panel configuration to be more practical: one required reviewer with two optional ones, and a strong arbiter.

## New defaults

\`\`\`
final_review.backends = ["codex/gpt-5.4-xhigh", "?claude-opus-4-6", "?codex/gpt-5.3-codex-spark-xhigh"]
final_review.min_reviewers = 1
final_review.arbiter_backend = codex/gpt-5.4-xhigh
\`\`\`

The \`?\` prefix means optional — if the backend is unavailable (exhausted, auth failure, etc.), the panel proceeds without it. Only the first backend (codex/gpt-5.4-xhigh) is required.

## Why

- The current default requires all 3 reviewers, which means a single backend auth failure kills the entire review
- gpt-5.4-xhigh is the most thorough reviewer (it finds real issues the others miss) — it should be required
- Claude and gpt-5.3-codex-spark are useful but not essential — making them optional improves resilience
- min_reviewers=1 means the panel can complete with just the required reviewer if others fail
- Codex 5.4 as arbiter ensures tie-breaking is done by the strongest model

## Dependencies

This should land AFTER the planner removal (ralph-burning-aro), since the planner config will no longer exist.

## Implementation hints

- Check how backend lists are parsed in the config system
- The \`?\` prefix for optional backends may need to be implemented if it doesn't exist yet
- Look at how final_review.backends is resolved in the stage resolution code
- min_reviewers should gate the minimum panel size for the review to proceed
- Update default config values in the workspace governance or config module

## Acceptance Criteria
- Default final review panel uses the new backend configuration
- Optional backends (prefixed with ?) degrade gracefully when unavailable
- min_reviewers=1 allows the panel to proceed with a single reviewer
- Arbiter defaults to codex/gpt-5.4-xhigh
- Existing explicit config overrides still work
- cargo test && cargo clippy && cargo fmt --check pass
- nix build . succeeds
acceptance_criteria: nix build passes on the final tree

title: Add run liveness tracking, stop command, and automatic stale-run recovery
description:
## Goal

Make run lifecycle robust against process death: track whether the orchestrator is alive, add a `run stop` command for graceful shutdown, and let `run status` and `run resume` automatically detect and recover from stale Running states.

## Problem

When the ralph-burning process is killed (SIGKILL, OOM, terminal closed), the run snapshot stays in `Running` status with `active_run` populated. This creates a stuck state that requires manual JSON surgery to fix:

1. `run resume` rejects `Running` status (line 1358 in engine.rs) — it only accepts `Failed` or `Paused`
2. `run status` reports "running" even though nothing is running
3. There is no `run stop` command for graceful shutdown
4. The journal reconciliation (line 1336) only catches the case where `fail_run` wrote a journal event but the snapshot write failed — it cannot detect a killed process

## Changes Required

### 1. PID/liveness file for the orchestrator

Write a `run.pid` (or similar) file in the live project root when a run starts. Contents: PID + start timestamp. On `run status` and `run resume`, check if the PID is still alive (`kill -0` or `/proc/<pid>/stat`).

### 2. `run stop` command

Add a `run stop` subcommand that:
- Reads the PID file
- Sends SIGTERM to the orchestrator process (and waits briefly for graceful shutdown)
- If the process doesn't exit, sends SIGKILL
- Transitions the snapshot from Running to Paused (or Failed) with `interrupted_run` populated
- Cleans up the PID file

### 3. Stale-run detection in `run status`

When status shows Running, check the PID file:
- PID alive → genuinely running, report normally
- PID dead or PID file missing → stale, report "stale (process not found)" and suggest `run resume`

### 4. Automatic recovery in `run resume`

When snapshot is Running and the PID is dead:
- Automatically transition to Failed (move active_run to interrupted_run)
- Log the reconciliation
- Continue with normal resume flow

This should replace the manual JSON surgery currently needed.

### 5. Signal handling in the orchestrator

Register a SIGTERM handler that:
- Sets the cancellation token (triggers graceful shutdown of current backend invocation)
- Writes a clean Paused/Failed snapshot before exiting
- This makes `run stop` → `run resume` a clean cycle

## Files likely affected

- `src/cli/run.rs` — add `stop` subcommand, enhance `status`
- `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs` — PID file write/check, signal handler, stale detection in resume
- `src/adapters/fs.rs` — PID file I/O helpers
- `src/contexts/project_run_record/model.rs` — possibly new status variant or liveness field

## Acceptance Criteria

- Orchestrator writes a PID file on run start, removes on clean exit
- `run stop` gracefully stops a running orchestrator and transitions to resumable state
- `run status` detects stale Running states (dead PID) and reports accurately
- `run resume` automatically recovers from stale Running states without manual JSON editing
- Signal handler ensures clean snapshot writes on SIGTERM
- Existing tests pass
- cargo test && cargo clippy && cargo fmt --check

title: Make status reconciliation attempt-aware to prevent stale terminal events from overriding live runs
description: Status reconciliation uses the journal's global last terminal event, but resumed attempts reuse the same run_id. A live resumed run still has an older run_failed for that run_id, so handle_status can incorrectly patch the current Running snapshot to Failed/Completed. The reconciliation needs to ignore terminal events that occurred before the most recent run_started/run_resumed boundary for the current attempt. Also handle the edge case where run_started is missing but a terminal event exists.

title: Make PID liveness safe across platforms and after PID reuse
description: Non-Linux builds only populate proc_start_marker but is_pid_alive only checks proc_start_ticks, making macOS/BSD runs unrecoverable from run.pid. Legacy pid files without proc_start_ticks are treated as stale even when the process is alive, or conversely treated as live after PID reuse via raw kill(pid,0). Need: (1) honor proc_start_marker on non-Linux, (2) treat missing start-time fields as unsafe/legacy, (3) compare live process start time against recorded started_at for legacy records to detect PID reuse.
acceptance_criteria: nix build passes on the final tree

title: Bind stale-run recovery to specific run attempt and revalidate before reclaiming
description: RunPidRecord stores process identity but not run_id/attempt metadata. A concurrent run resume can replace run.pid and the writer-lock owner after the stale snapshot was read. In that race, run stop can kill the new resumed orchestrator, and stale run resume can reclaim a fresh writer lease belonging to the new attempt. Fix: persist active run identity in run.pid, abort/retry when snapshot changed before signaling or reclaiming, revalidate that current owner/pid still belongs to the stale attempt before reclaiming.
acceptance_criteria: nix build passes on the final tree

title: Make SIGTERM cleanup durable and kill backend subprocess trees on forced stop
description: Signal wrapper only cancels the token and waits; it never writes an interrupted snapshot or removes run.pid itself. PID cleanup is deferred to the outer CLI epilogue. If cancellation hangs or the process dies after writing the failed snapshot but before reaching outer cleanup, the project is left stale. Also: run stop SIGKILL only kills the orchestrator PID but backends are in their own process groups (process_group(0)). After forced stop, orphan backend processes keep running. And on non-Linux Unix, the descendant-group cleanup helper is missing entirely. Fix: move interrupted snapshot + pid cleanup into the signal/fail path, signal backend process groups on forced stop across all Unix platforms.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add daemon-owned run SIGTERM cleanup and surface partial lease cleanup failures
description: Daemon dispatch path lacks the forced SIGTERM cleanup that the CLI wrapper now has. If the daemon is SIGKILLed by a supervisor because the backend ignored cancellation, the run is left stale with a lingering run.pid. Also: partial stale-owner cleanup (writer lock released but worktree/lease cleanup fails) is silently ignored before reacquiring the CLI writer lease, allowing resume/stop to continue on contradictory durable state. Fix: add equivalent termination cleanup to daemon loop, surface and block on partial cleanup failures.
close_reason: Implemented daemon SIGTERM handoff cleanup and fail-closed stale-owner cleanup

title: ralph pr open: validate real-code diff before destructive soft-reset
description:
PR #208 (2qlo) performs \`git reset --soft origin/master\` and *then* checks whether any review-scope diff remains, returning NoRealCodeChanges if the staged diff is metadata-only. The branch HEAD has already been rewritten by the time the error fires, so the user has to recover via reflog instead of getting a clean no-op failure.

Fix: peek at \`git diff origin/master..HEAD\` (filtered to review-scope paths) BEFORE the reset. Only reset when real-code is present. Document the flow in a comment.

Bot's review on PR #208: https://github.com/douglaz/ralph-burning/pull/208#discussion_r2522714

## Acceptance Criteria
close_reason: Landed via PR #217. iterative_minimal converged 0 amendments round 1.

title: ralph pr open: resolve base branch instead of hardcoding master
description:
PR #208 (2qlo) hardcodes \`master\`/\`origin/master\` in src/contexts/bead_workflow/pr_open.rs at multiple call sites (fetch, ancestry check, log walk, soft-reset, gh pr base). This breaks in repos whose default branch is main or any non-master name.

Fix: introduce a base-branch resolver on GitPort that runs \`git symbolic-ref --quiet --short refs/remotes/origin/HEAD\` and falls back to checking \`origin/main\` then \`origin/master\` (the same logic src/contexts/workflow_composition/final_review.rs::review_base_ref already uses). Cache the result on the GitPort instance; thread the resolved ref through the pr_open methods that currently take literal "master".

Bot's review on PR #208: https://github.com/douglaz/ralph-burning/pull/208#discussion_r2522715

## Acceptance Criteria
close_reason: Landed via PR #214. Produced by ralph drain --max-cycles 1 — first real-world e2e drain test. iterative_minimal converged round 1, 0 amendments. Drain process itself failed at the post-run push step (filed as w5da).

title: rb: checkpoint commits include the gitignored 'result' symlink
description:
## Observed behavior

During a ralph-burning run in bead-aware mode, the periodic `rb: checkpoint ...` commits created on the feature branch stage and commit the top-level `result` symlink (the nix build output). The symlink is listed in `.gitignore` (line 5), but because it is already tracked in the repository history, git continues to track changes to it, and the checkpoint logic has no filter to skip it.

Concrete trace from feat/uh1-cleanup-planner-refs (PR #168):
- `git log --oneline -- result` showed the symlink being changed in multiple `rb: checkpoint project=uh1-2 ...` commits across rounds.
- `.gitignore` contains `result` (line 5) yet `git check-ignore -v result` returns nothing because the path is already in the index.

Effect: every `nix build` between checkpoints flips the symlink target, those flips leak into PR diffs, and reviewers see spurious `result` changes unrelated to the bead's work.

## Expected behavior

Either:
1. `rb: checkpoint` should explicitly skip paths that are in .gitignore even when already tracked (whitelist src/ tests/ docs/ + .ralph-burning/ state, or blacklist known nix outputs like `result`, `result-*`).
2. Ralph-burning should `git rm --cached` once on the first checkpoint after detecting that a tracked path is gitignored, so subsequent runs do not touch it.
3. Alternatively: document the constraint and have the harness `git update-index --assume-unchanged result` before starting a run.

Option 2 or 3 is preferable because option 1 still leaves the repo in a weird state (tracked + gitignored) which causes other tooling to misbehave.

## Reproduce

1. On a clean checkout of ralph-burning master, note that `result` is tracked:
   ```
   git ls-files result
   # result
   git check-ignore -v result
   # (no output — because it is tracked)
   ```
2. Run `nix build` once to produce a `result` symlink.
3. Start any ralph-burning project run.
4. Wait for the first `rb: checkpoint` commit on the feature branch.
5. Observe that the commit diff includes `result`.

## Affected code

- Whatever code path builds the checkpoint commit in ralph-burning — likely in the `automation_runtime` or `workflow_composition` engines (search for 'rb: checkpoint' commit message template, or for `git add`/`git commit` calls in checkpoint logic).

## Acceptance criteria

- New PRs created via ralph-burning do not include `result` changes in their diffs unless the user explicitly stages it.
- `result` is either untracked (preferred) or its contents are deliberately excluded from checkpoint commits.
- `cargo test`, `cargo clippy --locked -- -D warnings`, `cargo fmt --check`, and `nix build` all still pass.
- A regression test or conformance check verifies that a checkpoint does not stage a gitignored path.

## Dogfood source

Discovered while driving bead ralph-burning-uh1 end-to-end with `ralph-burning` iterative_minimal flow on 2026-04-21. See PR #168 diff for evidence.

title: Make try_reconcile_success fail-closed when workflow_run_id is missing instead of guessing latest RunStarted
description:
## Goal

Replace the unsafe fallback in try_reconcile_success that guesses the latest RunStarted event when workflow_run_id is missing. This should fail closed with an operator handoff instead.

## Problem

In daemon_loop.rs (~line 1770-1825), try_reconcile_success falls back to the latest RunStarted journal event whenever workflow_run_id is missing on the task. Since persist_workflow_run_id is best-effort, a post-completion retry can legitimately reach this branch.

If someone manually reran the same project between the original failure and the retry, this code binds the daemon task to the newer run, then uses its RunCompleted timestamp and run ID for reconciliation. This can close/sync the bead and rewrite milestone lineage for the wrong attempt instead of surfacing a needs-operator condition.

## Fix

When workflow_run_id is None and persist_workflow_run_id failed or was skipped:
1. Transition the milestone to needs-operator (or Failed) with a descriptive reason
2. Return an error that marks the task as failed (retryable after operator intervention)
3. Do NOT guess the latest RunStarted — this is not safe

Alternatively, if a deterministic run binding can be established (e.g., matching run_id against task metadata like project_id + started_at window), that would also be acceptable.

## Files

- `src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs` — try_reconcile_success fallback path

## Acceptance Criteria

- Missing workflow_run_id does not silently bind to latest RunStarted
- Operator is signaled when run identity cannot be determined
- Task is marked failed so it can be retried after operator fixes the binding
- Existing tests pass; new test covers the missing-run-id path
acceptance_criteria: nix build passes on the final tree

title: Drop --output-schema for codex execution stages; synthesize payload from disk state
description:
## Goal

Eliminate the entire issue #188 bug class by removing codex's structured-output coupling. ralph already has the truth on disk (`diff_changed` from git) and doesn't actually use the model's structured payload to drive any orchestration decision. The structured payload is decorative — persisted to disk and shown to reviewers, but reviewers cite source lines, not the JSON summary.

PR #189 (`minItems: 1`), PR #193 (runtime detector), PR #194 (`contains`, broke master), and PR #195 (enum narrowing, the current attempt) all try to keep codex's `--output-schema` matcher honest. The simpler answer: stop using `--output-schema` for codex execution stages and let ralph synthesize the `ExecutionPayload`-shaped record from disk state itself.

## Why this works

- `diff_changed` (git diff before/after the turn) is filesystem ground truth and already drives ralph's iterative loop.
- final_review reviewers cite source lines from the actual diff, not the model's JSON summary (confirmed via the artifacts read during ni85-4 review).
- The codex transcript at `~/.codex/sessions/.../rollout-*.jsonl` is already on disk — copy it verbatim for forensics; no parsing required.
- Claude's path is untouched; it keeps `--json-schema` and the typed contract because Claude doesn't have the early-termination problem.

## Implementation sketch

1. `src/adapters/process_backend.rs::codex_new_session_args` and `codex_resume_args`: drop the `--output-schema` arg for Execution-family stage contracts (keep for Planning/Validation if those still need it; in scope for this bead to evaluate).
2. After the codex turn, in the codex `finish()` path: detect Execution family + codex backend + missing schema → synthesize an `ExecutionPayload` value:
   - `change_summary`: take the last assistant message text (truncate if huge) OR fixed string `"codex implementation turn completed (exit 0); see transcript"`.
   - `steps`: one synthetic `{order: 1, description: "codex implementation turn", status: "completed"}`. The forward-progress contract validator from PR #193 passes by construction.
   - `validation_evidence`: empty, or list any `exec_command` events with exit_code=0 from the transcript (informational).
   - `outstanding_risks`: empty.
3. Copy the codex session transcript JSONL into `<project>/history/transcripts/codex-<run_id>-<stage>-<cycle>-<round>.jsonl` so the run's history is self-contained.
4. Drop the `detect_codex_interim_execution_payload` runtime detector (PR #193) — without a schema there's no interim shape to detect.
5. Drop the codex-only `inject_codex_steps_status_enum` (PR #195) — same reason.
6. Drop the `Execution::validate_semantics` forward-progress check from PR #193, OR keep it; either way it's satisfied by the synthesized payload.

## What we lose

- The structured `validation_evidence` claim from the model. (We always knew it was unverifiable; codex was happy to fabricate placeholder strings.)
- The model's self-described `steps` list. Replaced with a transcript pointer that's strictly more useful for forensics.

## What we gain

- Issue #188 disappears entirely. No schema = no `--output-schema` matcher = no interim early-termination.
- ~250 lines of strict-mode-constraint-juggling code (`enforce_strict_mode_schema`, `inject_codex_steps_status_enum`, `detect_codex_interim_execution_payload`) become irrelevant for the codex Execution path. Some can be removed; the rest get cleaner because they're only relevant to non-codex or non-Execution contexts.
- Trust boundary moves to the right place: ralph trusts git, not the model.

## Acceptance criteria

- Codex implementer runs (e.g. `gpt-5.5-high` against any iterative_minimal flow) no longer hit issue #188 — verifiable by re-running the master-t9j0 reproducer mentioned in the issue.
- Existing Claude execution flows unaffected (regression: invoke an execution stage with `workflow.implementer_backend = claude` and verify the typed payload still flows end-to-end).
- New regression test: assert that `processed_contract_schema_value` for a codex Execution stage is no longer attached to the codex invocation (or that `codex_new_session_args` for Execution doesn't include `--output-schema`).
- New regression test: stub-backend codex execution invocation produces a synthesized `ExecutionPayload` with `status: completed` and the transcript file copied into the run's history.
- nix build passes; `cargo test --features test-stub --locked && cargo clippy --locked -- -D warnings && cargo fmt --check` pass.
- Once landed, close issue #188 and `ralph-burning-i59` (the session-resume follow-up) since both are obviated.

## IMPORTANT: Exclude orchestration state from review scope

Files under `.ralph-burning/` are live orchestration state and MUST NOT be reviewed or flagged. Only review code under `src/` and `tests/`.

title: final_review: retry on transient backend errors instead of failing the stage
description:
## Observed behavior

When the only viable final_review reviewer hits a transient backend error (e.g., OpenAI stream disconnect, 5xx, network blip), ralph-burning records the reviewer as `outcome=failed amendments=0` and immediately fails the stage / run instead of retrying the same backend.

Concrete trace from project ni95-1 on bead ralph-burning-9ni.9.5 (small CLI-help-text bead, completion round 2):

- reviewer-2 (codex/gpt-5.3-codex-spark-xhigh) failed_exhausted in 3.3s (credits — common).
- reviewer-1 (codex/gpt-5.4-xhigh) ran ~4 minutes then errored mid-stream:
  `backend invocation failed for contract 'final_review:reviewer' via 'codex': codex exited with code 1: ... ERROR: stream disconnected before completion ... Please include the request ID d65fabb3-a03f-43be-a5d4-90f7964b14ee in your message.`
- ralph-burning recorded:
  `final_review reviewer reviewer-1 proposal completed in 254029ms outcome=failed amendments=0`
  followed by `RunFailed`.

The error is **transient** (OpenAI's own message says 'You can retry your request'), so the right behavior is to retry the same reviewer (with backoff) before treating the stage as failed.

## Why this matters

Ralph-burning's stated graceful-degradation contract for final_review is 'proceed with remaining backends, fail only if all are exhausted.' That contract is hollow when the panel is already down to one live reviewer (because the others were exhausted/disabled) — a single transient OpenAI hiccup turns into a complete stage failure and a force-restart of the run.

This was triggered concretely by removing claude from `final_review.backends` (now `['codex/gpt-5.4-xhigh', '?codex/gpt-5.3-codex-spark-xhigh']`). The single-reviewer path is reachable in any panel where exhaustion drains optional reviewers, even with the default config — it is not a configuration mistake by the operator.

## Expected behavior

Treat backend errors classified as transient (network, 5xx, stream disconnect, rate-limit-with-retry-after, codex exit code with no usable output and an OpenAI 'retry your request' message in stderr) the same way the implementer stage already does:

- Apply `retry_policy` for the reviewer role (default already exists for transport failures): N retries with exponential backoff before the reviewer is recorded as failed.
- Only after the policy's max attempts are exhausted should the reviewer be marked `outcome=failed` and the stage advance to the 'remaining backends' fallback path.
- Distinguish `failure_class=transport_failure` (retryable) from `failure_class=domain_validation_failure` (not retryable — the model returned valid output that didn't satisfy the contract).

## Where to look

- `src/contexts/workflow_composition/final_review.rs` — reviewer / arbiter invocation site. Compare the implementer path in `engine.rs` which already uses `apply_retry_policy` / `is_timeout_related` heuristics.
- `src/adapters/process_backend.rs` — `is_timeout_related` (helper extracted in bead hg4) is the model for a similar `is_transient_codex_failure` helper that classifies stream disconnects, exit-1 with retry hints in stderr, and rate-limit signals.
- `src/contexts/workflow_composition/retry_policy.rs` — make sure the reviewer role uses the policy's transport_failure max_attempts (currently 5) instead of bypassing the policy.

## Acceptance criteria

- A reviewer or arbiter invocation that fails with a transport-classified error retries up to the configured `retry_policy` for that role before the reviewer is marked failed.
- An OpenAI 'stream disconnected before completion' / 'retry your request' message in the codex stderr is classified as transport_failure (not as a domain validation failure).
- A 1-reviewer panel where the single reviewer hits one transient error and succeeds on retry now completes the stage instead of failing the run.
- A 1-reviewer panel where the single reviewer hits N transient errors in a row (exhausting the policy) still fails the stage with a clear error indicating exhaustion of retries, not a single attempt.
- New unit tests in the relevant final_review test module covering: (a) one transient error then success, (b) all attempts transient errors → stage fails, (c) domain_validation_failure → no retry (current behavior preserved).
- `nix build`, `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy --locked -- -D warnings`, and `cargo test --locked --features test-stub` all pass.

## Dogfood source

Discovered while driving bead ralph-burning-9ni.9.5 with `ralph-burning` iterative_minimal on 2026-04-23 (project ni95-1, run failed at completion round 2, request ID d65fabb3-a03f-43be-a5d4-90f7964b14ee). After resuming, the same backend succeeded on the very next attempt — confirming the error was transient.

title: Change default implementer to Codex gpt-5.4 high and set reviewers to gpt-5.4 xhigh + Opus 4.6
description: Current defaults use Opus for implementation and Codex+Opus for review. Change to: implementer defaults to Codex gpt-5.4 with reasoning_effort=high (faster, cheaper for implementation), reviewers default to Codex gpt-5.4 with reasoning_effort=xhigh plus Opus 4.6 (independent cross-model review). This balances speed/cost for implementation with thoroughness for review. Should be configurable via workspace config to override.

title: Fix fail_run snapshot write failure leaving run permanently unrecoverable
description:
## Goal

Ensure a failed run can always be resumed or reported correctly, even when the snapshot write in `fail_run()` itself fails.

## Problem

`fail_run()` (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:5133-5144`) now ignores `write_run_snapshot()` failures and still records `run_failed` in the journal. If the snapshot write keeps failing:

- `run.json` stays in its previous `Running` state
- `resume_run_with_retry_internal()` (engine.rs:1147-1158) refuses to resume anything except `Failed` or `Paused` snapshots
- The run is permanently unrecoverable — it's failed per the journal but Running per the snapshot

## Fix Options

1. Make the snapshot write a hard failure in `fail_run()` (revert the change)
2. Make resume/start/status paths fall back to the journal when the snapshot is stale
3. Add a recovery path that detects journal says "failed" but snapshot says "running" and reconciles

## Files

- `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs` — `fail_run()`, `resume_run_with_retry_internal()`
close_reason: Fixed: journal-based reconciliation for stale snapshots after fail_run write failure

title: Drain harness: recompute ready beads from persisted graph each cycle
description:
The drain harness in src/test_support/drain_harness.rs (added by xrwl/PR #212)
walks a fixed bead_order captured at construction time, so beads filed via
file_follow_up_bead during the harness run are never picked up by subsequent
cycles. This diverges from production br ready behavior and means
recovery-path scenarios (e.g. BackendExhaustedSkip) can pass for the wrong
reason: the follow-up bead is invisible, so the test asserts "drain
continued" without actually exercising the case where a freshly-filed
follow-up gets queued.

Fix: have the harness re-read .beads/issues.jsonl each cycle (or wrap a
mock BrAdapter whose ready output reflects writes back to the file).
The production br adapter does this naturally; the harness should mirror
that.

Bot's review on PR #212: https://github.com/douglaz/ralph-burning/pull/212#discussion_r2522932

## Acceptance Criteria
close_reason: Landed via PR #219. iterative_minimal converged 1, 0.

title: ralph drain: top-level bead-queue drain loop
description:
Compose rb-drain.1, .2, .3, .4 into a continuous drain over br ready.

Each cycle:
1. Sync master (git fetch && git reset --hard origin/master), nix build sanity check
2. Pick top of br ready (skip beads with status != open)
3. ralph bead create-project <id> (rb-drain.2)
4. Mark bead in_progress
5. ralph run start (the existing command)
6. Poll run status until completed/failed
7. On completed: ralph pr open (rb-drain.3) → ralph pr watch (rb-drain.4) → on merge: br close <id>
8. On failed: hand off per rb-drain.6 policy
9. Loop

Stop conditions (configurable):
- Queue empty (br ready returns nothing)
- N consecutive bead failures (default 3)
- A P0 bead appears in the queue (preempt for dogfood-incident handling)
- User interrupt (SIGINT, ralph drain stop)

Reporting:
- Per-cycle one-line summary (bead id, convergence pattern, PR number, outcome, duration)
- End-of-loop summary (beads landed, failed, skipped)

This is the headline command this whole bead chain is for. Implementation can be a thin orchestrator that shells out to the underlying commands rather than re-implementing them inline.

Depends on: rb-drain.2, rb-drain.3, rb-drain.4, rb-drain.6.

## Acceptance Criteria
close_reason: Landed via PR #211. iterative_minimal ran 7 rounds with convergence 3,2,2,2,3,2,1 — force-shipped per gj74's documented escape hatch. The headline ralph drain command exists.

title: Document crash-durability limitations of atomic hint file write
description:
## Goal

Document that the atomic write pattern in persist_next_step_hint protects against torn reads but not crash durability without fsync on the parent directory.

## Problem

In success_reconciliation.rs (persist_next_step_hint), the write-to-tmp + rename pattern is correct for POSIX atomicity (no torn reads of truncated JSON). However, on some Linux filesystems the rename is only durable after an fsync on the parent directory. Without fsync, a crash after rename could lose the file entry entirely.

Since hints are best-effort and non-blocking, this is acceptable behavior, but it should be explicitly documented.

## Fix

Add a comment in persist_next_step_hint noting that the atomicity guarantee covers torn-read prevention, not crash durability, and that this is intentional given the best-effort nature of hints.

## Files

- `src/contexts/automation_runtime/success_reconciliation.rs`

## Acceptance Criteria

- Comment documents the atomicity vs durability tradeoff
- No functional change

title: vl8z: drain doesn't invoke pr open/pr watch — tries to push beads to master directly
description:
The ralph drain command (vl8z) was supposed to compose pr_open (2qlo) + pr_watch (itn1) into the success-path completion of a drain cycle. In practice it doesn't.

Observed in the first real-world end-to-end test on bead sr2x:

1. drain picked sr2x, marked it in_progress.
2. drain created the feature branch feat/ralph-burning-sr2x-ralph-pr-open-resolve-base-branch.
3. drain ran iterative_minimal — converged round 1, 0 amendments.
4. The run produced 352 lines of real code on the feature branch (including the actual sr2x implementation: base-branch resolver in pr_open.rs).
5. drain then switched back to master.
6. drain tried git push origin HEAD:master with only beads/ changes — blocked by master branch protection.
7. drain failed with the "GH013: repository rule violations" error and filed a follow-up bead.

The actual code changes from the run never got pushed or opened as a PR. The feature branch sits there with checkpoint commits but no PR.

Looking at src/cli/drain.rs:455, drain just does git push origin HEAD:master after a "sync beads after drain" commit. There's no integration with src/contexts/bead_workflow/pr_open.rs (2qlo) or pr_watch.rs (itn1).

The drain harness (xrwl) tests pass because they mock at a level above pr_open/pr_watch — they don't catch this gap.

Fix: replace the direct git push with calls to:
- bead_workflow::pr_open::run_pr_open (squash + push + create PR via gh)
- bead_workflow::pr_watch::watch_pr (poll CI + bot, merge on green + +1)

These already exist as library functions; vl8z just needs to invoke them.

## Acceptance Criteria
close_reason: Landed via PR #215. iterative_minimal converged 1,0. Drain now invokes pr_open+pr_watch on success; bead-sync PR failures warn-and-continue; just-landed beads are filtered from next-cycle re-pick.

title: Skip ApplyFixes stage when review has no findings or amendments
description:
## Goal

Make the ApplyFixes stage conditional — skip it entirely when the preceding Review stage approved with zero findings and zero amendments. Currently ApplyFixes runs unconditionally, wasting ~2+ minutes and a backend call just to re-run tests when there's nothing to fix.

## Problem

In the quick_dev flow (PlanAndImplement → Review → ApplyFixes → FinalReview), when Review approves with no findings, ApplyFixes does nothing useful — it just re-validates that tests still pass. This was observed across 9+ consecutive rounds on bead 9ni.2.4 where Review always approved with zero amendments and ApplyFixes produced "No fixes required" every time.

## Proposed Solution

In the engine's stage progression logic, after Review completes:
1. Check the Review stage artifact/outcome for findings and amendments
2. If the review outcome is "Approved" with empty findings and no follow-up amendments, skip the ApplyFixes stage and advance directly to the next stage (e.g. FinalReview)
3. If the review has any findings, amendments, or non-approved outcome, run ApplyFixes as normal

This should work generically for any flow that includes both Review and ApplyFixes stages, not just quick_dev.

## Key Files

- `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs` — stage progression loop, where stage skipping logic would be added
- `src/contexts/workflow_composition/mod.rs` — flow semantics (execution_stage currently points to ApplyFixes for quick_dev; may need adjustment)

## Acceptance Criteria

- ApplyFixes is skipped when Review approved with zero findings and zero amendments
- ApplyFixes still runs when Review has findings or amendments
- Works for any flow that contains Review → ApplyFixes in its stage sequence
- Stage skipping is visible in runtime logs (e.g. "skipping apply_fixes: review approved with no findings")
- No impact on flows that don't include ApplyFixes
- Existing tests pass; new tests cover the skip/no-skip logic
close_reason: Merged in PR #97

title: Gracefully degrade when a backend exhausts credits or hits persistent rate limits
description:
## Goal

When a backend (e.g. Codex/GPT-5.4) hits a persistent error like "credits exhausted" or "usage limit reached", the system should degrade gracefully instead of failing the entire run after exhausting retries.

## Problem

Observed on bead gn8: Codex exited with code 1 and stderr containing:

```
ERROR: You've hit your usage limit. Visit https://chatgpt.com/codex/settings/usage to purchase more credits or try again at 3:03 PM.
```

This was classified as `TransportFailure` (retryable, 5 attempts). The system retried 5 times, failed every time (credits don't come back by retrying), and killed the entire run — even though the other reviewer (Claude Opus) was working fine.

Increasing retry limits (bead gn8) doesn't help here because the error is persistent, not transient.

## Changes Required

### 1. Detect persistent backend unavailability

**File:** `src/adapters/process_backend.rs`

Parse stderr/stdout for known persistent error patterns:
- "usage limit" / "hit your usage limit"
- "quota exceeded"
- "billing" / "credits"
- "try again at <time>"

Introduce a new `FailureClass::BackendUnavailable` (or similar) that is distinct from `TransportFailure`. This class should NOT be retried blindly.

### 2. Graceful panel degradation for final_review

**File:** `src/contexts/workflow_composition/final_review.rs`

When a reviewer in the final_review panel fails with `BackendUnavailable`:
- Skip that reviewer for this round
- Proceed with remaining reviewer(s) if at least one is available
- Log a warning: "reviewer codex/gpt-5.4 unavailable (credits exhausted), proceeding with 1/2 reviewers"
- Adjust consensus threshold accordingly (or require unanimous from remaining)

### 3. Optional: wait-until-reset

If the error message contains a "try again at <time>" hint, the system could optionally:
- Parse the reset time
- Sleep until that time and retry
- This should be configurable (default: skip, not wait)

### 4. Apply to all multi-backend stages

The degradation logic should work for any stage that uses multiple backends (final_review panel, voter panel), not just final_review reviewers.

## Acceptance Criteria

- "Credits exhausted" errors are detected and classified as BackendUnavailable
- Final review panel proceeds with available reviewers when one backend is unavailable
- Run does NOT fail when a non-critical backend is unavailable
- Warning is logged when a backend is skipped
- If ALL backends are unavailable, the run fails with a clear error message
- Existing tests pass; new tests cover degradation scenarios
close_reason: Implemented: BackendExhausted failure class with panel degradation. Leftover amendments filed as p2h and gsp.

title: End-to-end drain harness test (subsumes 9ni.10.2)

title: Fix 5 nix-build test failures: tests depend on ps binary unavailable in nix sandbox
description:
5 tests fail during nix build because they call `ps` to enumerate process groups, which is not available in the nix sandbox environment.

## Failing tests
1. `cli::run::tests::kill_stale_backend_process_group_refuses_exited_authoritative_leader_without_safe_proof`
2. `cli::run::tests::repair_missing_interrupted_handoff_run_failed_event_and_reload_snapshot_cleans_stale_daemon_backends`
3. `contexts::automation_runtime::daemon_loop::tests::load_dispatch_run_snapshot_repairs_missing_run_failed_event_from_daemon_handoff`
4. `contexts::automation_runtime::daemon_loop::tests::process_cycle_multi_repo_blocks_repo_after_recovered_terminal_cleanup_failure`
5. `contexts::automation_runtime::daemon_loop::tests::process_cycle_phase0_repairs_persisted_cancelled_handoff_for_aborted_task`

## Root cause
These tests exercise process-group enumeration via `ps` (called in the stale-run recovery and daemon handoff paths). The `ps` binary exists in normal environments but is not available inside the nix build sandbox, causing `No such file or directory (os error 2)`.

## Error pattern
\`\`\`
failed to enumerate process groups via ps: No such file or directory (os error 2)
\`\`\`

## Fix options
1. Add ps (procps) to nix build sandbox via nativeBuildInputs in flake.nix (simplest)
2. Mock or stub the ps call in tests so they dont depend on the host binary
3. Skip these tests in the nix sandbox with a feature flag or environment check (least desirable)

## Acceptance Criteria
- nix build . succeeds (844 passed, 0 failed)
- No test regressions in nix develop -c cargo test
- cargo clippy and cargo fmt --check pass

title: Migrate the project to Rust 2024 edition
description:
Goal:
Adopt the Rust 2024 edition for the workspace and make the codebase and build tooling fully compatible with it.

title: Add iterative_minimal workflow: loop implementer until stable, then review
description:
## Goal

Add a new workflow preset similar to minimal, but with an iterative implementer loop. The implementer is called repeatedly until its changes stabilize, rather than once per round. This trades API cost for deeper implementation work.

## Motivation

The existing minimal flow calls the implementer once per round, applies amendments, then calls again. Some tasks benefit from letting the implementer iterate on its own work multiple times before review — the model often spots its own bugs or incomplete pieces if given another turn. This workflow formalizes that pattern.

## New workflow: iterative_minimal

### Phase 1: Implementer loop
1. Call implementer with original prompt (and amendments if this is a review cycle)
2. Record the diff produced
3. Call implementer again, asking it to continue/refine
4. Stop looping when EITHER:
   - Two consecutive implementer calls produce NO code changes (diff is empty)
   - Max consecutive rounds reached (configurable, default 10)

### Phase 2: Final review panel
- Runs the usual final review panel (same as minimal)
- Reviewers vote on amendments
- Arbiter resolves split votes

### Phase 3: Re-enter Phase 1 with amendments
- If amendments are accepted, return to Phase 1
- Implementer sees original prompt + amendments
- Same stop conditions apply

### Phase 4: Convergence
- Complete when final_review produces zero amendments
- Or hits max_completion_rounds

## Configuration

New settings:
- `workflow.iterative_minimal.max_consecutive_implementer_rounds` (default: 10)
- `workflow.iterative_minimal.stable_rounds_required` (default: 2) — how many consecutive empty-diff rounds trigger early exit

## Implementation pointers

- Add FlowPreset::IterativeMinimal variant in `src/shared/domain.rs`
- Define flow stages in the workflow composition engine
- The plan_and_implement stage logic needs a loop variant; check `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs`
- Track diff-before / diff-after for each implementer invocation to detect empty diffs
- Emit journal events per implementer iteration (so operators can see loop progress)
- Use existing checkpoint/rollback infrastructure between iterations

## Journal events

New event types (or new role on existing events):
- `implementer_iteration_started` with iteration number
- `implementer_iteration_completed` with diff_size, accumulated_diff_size
- `implementer_loop_exited` with reason (stable | max_rounds | error)

## UX

- `run tail` should show "implementer iteration N/10" progress
- `run status` should show the iteration count when in implementer loop

## Acceptance Criteria
- FlowPreset::IterativeMinimal selectable via --flow iterative_minimal
- Implementer loops with correct stop conditions
- Empty-diff detection works (git-based comparison)
- Max rounds respected (default 10, configurable)
- Final review panel integrates cleanly, same quorum/arbiter behavior as minimal
- Amendments re-enter the implementer loop correctly
- Unit tests for stop-condition logic
- Integration test for full cycle with stubbed implementer
- Journal events emitted per iteration
- cargo test && cargo clippy -- -D warnings && cargo fmt --check pass
- nix build passes on the final tree

title: Check project ID availability before claiming bead in create-from-bead
description: handle_create_from_bead claims the bead in br (br update --status=in_progress) before project creation, but duplicate project ID validation only happens inside create_project_from_bead_context. If --project-id collides with an existing project, the bead is left claimed with no linked task and the controller moves to needs_operator, requiring manual recovery for a simple input error. Fix: check project ID existence before claim_bead_in_br so preventable errors fail fast without side effects. Found in PR #107 review.
close_reason: implemented: check project ID before claiming, merged in PR #108

title: ralph pr open: fetch remote refs before resolving base branch
description:
Bot finding on PR #214 (sr2x): the new BaseBranch resolver in pr_open.rs only inspects local refs (origin/HEAD, origin/main, origin/master). In repos where origin exists but no refs/remotes/origin/* have been materialized locally (e.g. fresh clone with origin added but never fetched), resolution fails before any git fetch happens. Previously master-based repos could recover via git fetch origin master.

Fix: do a git fetch origin (or git fetch origin --no-tags --filter=blob:none with a default-ref query) before base_branch_ref's local-ref resolution. The fetch is idempotent and cheap if refs are up to date.

## Acceptance Criteria
close_reason: Landed via PR #216. iterative_minimal converged 0 amendments round 1.

title: Stabilize check_available_times_out_when_version_probe_hangs (50ms timeout race)
description:
This test in src/adapters/br_process.rs:2127 spawns a fake-br binary that
hangs on --version, then asserts check_available_with_timeout(50ms) returns
BrTimeout. On loaded CI runners the spawn itself can exceed 50ms, producing
a different error class (likely BrExitError or pipe-related) and the
matches!(error, BrError::BrTimeout {..}) assertion fails.

Failure mode observed in https://github.com/douglaz/ralph-burning/actions/runs/25098402297 (PR #200 amended push). Test passes locally and on most CI runs; rerun typically succeeds.

Fix options:
- Bump the timeout to 200-500ms (more headroom for spawn under load)
- Or assert on either BrTimeout OR a transient error class
- Or pre-warm the fake-br binary (e.g., a no-op invocation) before timing the hung one
